sound_type: shrieking locked-talon tumble -- two eagles grapple talons in aerial territorial display; spin and cartwheel in freefall from height; continuous high-pitched screaming throughout; separation before impact; audible from hundreds of meters
ecosystem: Territory -- riparian corridors, lake shorelines, coastal areas; nesting territory defense; year-round in prime Pacific Northwest habitat
season: Resident (year-round; locked-talon combat most frequent late winter to early spring when breeding investment is maximum)
conservation_status: Least Concern (IUCN). Delisted from US Federal Endangered Species List 2007. Successful recovery from DDT-related population decline. Washington State has one of the highest populations in the contiguous US.
milestone: Second Accipitridae entry in SPS engine. Return from degree 221 (contact call, E=5). Behavioral escalation: contact call (E=5) to locked-talon aerial combat (E=7). Two-level energy escalation for the same species across different behaviors. Opens raptor cluster at degrees 297-299.

coordinates.description: Pacific Northwest distribution: Skagit River valley (December-January chum salmon concentration, hundreds of eagles), San Juan Islands, Puget Sound shorelines, coastal rivers. Year-round resident in prime habitat. Skagit River Bald Eagle Natural Area is the most significant concentration site.

acoustic_data.vocalization_type: Territorial combat screaming -- the contact call (kee-kee-kee) pushed to maximum urgency and volume; continuous bilateral screaming from both combatants throughout the locked-talon display; carries hundreds of meters.
acoustic_data.locked_talon_mechanics: Two eagles grip each other's feet with talons; combined weight produces tumbling freefall; cartwheeling motion from aerodynamic forces; documented freefall up to 100+ feet; separation before impact requires precise timing; failure to separate risks injury or drowning.
acoustic_data.e7_calibration: E=7: maximum territorial behavioral commitment. Physical risk accepted. Bilateral sustained screaming throughout display. The highest-intensity Bald Eagle behavior. Two levels above the contact call at E=5.

nodes.title: E=5 to E=7 -- Contact Call to Aerial Combat
nodes.description: Same species, two behaviors, two energy levels. Contact call (221, E=5): ambient communication. Locked-talon combat (297, E=7): maximum territorial commitment. The energy gap accurately represents the behavioral gap. The engine's most precise single-species energy calibration.

nodes.title: Raptor Cluster 297-299
nodes.description: Three consecutive raptor/owl degrees approaching the five-sixths mark: Bald Eagle combat (297, E=7, Accipitridae), Northern Goshawk alarm (298, E=7, Accipitridae), Great Horned Owl territorial (299, E=6, Strigidae). Then Pileated Woodpecker (300, E=7, Picidae) at the milestone. The ecological cluster matches the S36 return cluster in intensity.

nodes.title: Return from Degree 221 -- FIRST ACCIPITRIDAE
nodes.description: Degree 221 was the first Accipitridae entry in the engine: Bald Eagle contact call, E=5, paired with Love Battery (U-District enters). The return at 297 escalates to E=7. The Accipitridae family documents its full behavioral range through two entries of the same species.

cross_references.relationship: First appearance E=5. FIRST ACCIPITRIDAE. Contact call only. E=5 to E=7 behavioral escalation on return.
Second Accipitridae E=7. Raptor cluster continues at 298. Two consecutive Accipitridae both at E=7.
E=7 + E=7 = E=14. Matched energy. Proto-grunge riff architecture and locked-talon combat: sustained structural effort at altitude.
